data.title: Wall Street Holds Emergency Meeting: Claude's New Model Poses Huge Risks to the Financial Industry
data.description:
Author: Long Yue, Wall Street Insights

Anthropic's latest AI model, Mythos, has triggered high alert from US regulators—two key figures in US financial regulation have joined forces to urgently summon CEOs of top Wall Street banks to Washington.

According to the latest report from Bloomberg, US Treasury Secretary Bessant and Federal Reserve Chairman Powell urgently convened a meeting with CEOs of major Wall Street banks at the Treasury Department headquarters in Washington on Tuesday.The core topic of the meeting was the potential cybersecurity threats posed by Anthropic's latest AI model, Mythos.

The fact that this meeting was arranged on short notice and not publicly reported beforehand is itself a signal: regulators consider new types of cyberattacks to be one of the biggest risks currently facing the financial industry. All invited banks are classified as systemically important financial institutions by top regulatory agencies, and their stability is directly related to the global financial system. Who came, who didn't come? According to sources who spoke to the media, the bank CEOs attending the meeting included: Jane Fraser of Citigroup, Ted Pick of Morgan Stanley, Brian Moynihan of Bank of America, Charlie Scharf of Wells Fargo, and David Solomon of Goldman Sachs. JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon was absent. Spokespeople for all banks declined to comment, and an Anthropic representative did not immediately respond. This lineup almost encompasses the largest systemically important banks in the United States. The regulators' choice to speak directly with the CEO level, rather than at the technical or compliance level, demonstrates the urgency of this warning. Just how dangerous is Mythos? According to Anthropic, Mythos can, under user instructions, "identify and exploit vulnerabilities in every major operating system and web browser." This means that if this model falls into the hands of malicious actors, its attack surface will cover almost all digital infrastructure. For banks heavily reliant on digital systems, this is not an abstract risk—once a core system is compromised, transaction disruptions, data breaches, and even systemic chain reactions are all possible. From real-world data, Mythos's capabilities are not exaggerated. Previously reported, the model discovered a 27-year-old remote crash vulnerability in OpenBSD and a 16-year-old vulnerability in FFmpeg that had been scanned over 5 million times by automated tools but never triggered an alert. Furthermore, it autonomously chained multiple vulnerabilities in the Linux kernel, constructing a complete attack chain from escalating ordinary user privileges to full machine control. For this reason, Anthropic has adopted an extremely cautious approach to its release strategy, currently only opening it to a select few leading technology and financial institutions, including Amazon, Apple, and JPMorgan Chase. These institutions are jointly participating in "Project Glasswing," which aims to be the first to harden the security of critical systems before similar AI models become more widely available. Anthropic stated that it had communicated with U.S. officials regarding Mythos's "offensive and defensive cyber capabilities" prior to the recent release. "Too Powerful" to Be Publicly Discreet: "Project Glasswing" Defenders Take the Lead. As previously mentioned, on Tuesday, April 7th, Anthropic announced the establishment of an industry consortium called "Project Glasswing," collaborating with companies such as Amazon, Apple, Microsoft, and Cisco to provide tools and utilize its next-generation cutting-edge model, Claude Mythos Preview, for vulnerability scanning and remediation of critical software infrastructure. Anthropic stated that due to the immense power of this model, there are currently no plans to release it to the public. Against this backdrop, Project Glasswing is positioned as a preemptive defensive operation, prioritizing its use for defensive purposes before equivalent capabilities are disseminated to a wider range of actors. Anthropic stated that it had been in ongoing communication with US government officials regarding its "cybersecurity capabilities" prior to the release of Mythos. JPMorgan Chase Chief Information Security Officer Pat Opet stated that the bank will evaluate the tool's value in defending critical financial infrastructure in a "rigorous and independent manner." It is worth noting that Anthropic is currently embroiled in a legal dispute with the Trump administration. The Pentagon designated Anthropic as a supply chain risk, a designation Anthropic disputed. This week, a federal appeals court rejected Anthropic's request to suspend the designation. This context makes the relationship between regulators and Anthropic more delicate—on the one hand, the Treasury and the Federal Reserve view its model as a systemic risk requiring urgent attention; on the other hand, Anthropic still faces legal pressure at the government level.

data.title: Binance Launches Zero-Fee Prediction Markets, Letting Users Trade Outcomes for Free
data.description:
Binance Bets On “Free” Trading To Capture The $20 Billion Market Share

Binance is making a high-impact entry into the prediction markets sector, integrating event-based trading into its Wallet app while eliminating trading and settlement fees. The move positions the exchange to compete directly with incumbents like Polymarket and Kalshi in a rapidly expanding market now handling billions in monthly volume.

Binance just killed every barrier to prediction markets in one move.

Gas fees, bridging, wallet setup, desktop-only access - gone.@BinanceWallet × @predictdotfun, a full prediction market now lives inside the Binance App.

→ Gas fees? Binance covers 100%. You pay nothing. Not… https://t.co/QxvXyXy4Ujpic.twitter.com/Cp1smHGNnT

— shmidt (@shmidtqq) April 9, 2026

At the core of Binance’s push is a simple but aggressive strategy: remove friction entirely. Through its integration with Predict.fun, the platform allows users to trade on outcome-based markets without paying gas, trading, or settlement fees, effectively creating a zero-cost entry point for retail participants.

Built on the BNB Smart Chain, the system enables users to speculate on real-world events ranging from politics to sports, while Binance absorbs the underlying transaction costs. The “gasless” experience removes one of the biggest barriers in onchain trading, particularly for newer users unfamiliar with blockchain mechanics.

Stake your take across crypto, politics, sports, and more.

Prediction Markets are now live, thanks to @predictdotfun’s seamless integration with Binance Wallet.

Learn more 👉 https://t.co/NhmLXYLqR8pic.twitter.com/bBG5cUcmLD

— Binance (@binance) April 9, 2026

This model mirrors tactics previously used by exchanges to bootstrap liquidity, but its application in prediction markets marks a notable escalation. By subsidizing all transaction costs, Binance is not only lowering the barrier to entry but also directly undercutting competitors that rely on fee-based models.

Binance’s timing coincides with a surge in prediction market activity, with platforms like Polymarket seeing volumes climb to an estimated $20 billion in a single month during peak periods. The growth has been fueled by rising interest in event-based trading tied to elections, geopolitics, and macroeconomic developments.

Prediction markets are on fire 🔥

Polymarket just surpassed Kalshi in weekly volume and is reportedly valued around $20B.

Largest platforms by weekly volume:
1.Polymarket — $1.93B
2.Kalshi — $1.87B
3.Probable — $133M
4.Opinion — $132M
5.Predict Fun — $55M pic.twitter.com/YdV945qLws

— Nabil Abdullah (@NabilMinhaz) April 5, 2026

By embedding prediction markets directly into its Wallet app, Binance is effectively turning its massive user base into a distribution channel for event contracts. This gives it a structural advantage over standalone platforms, which must acquire users independently while navigating regulatory uncertainty.

The zero-fee model further intensifies competition. While existing platforms have focused on liquidity and market depth, Binance is targeting accessibility and scale, aiming to onboard a broader retail audience through a frictionless trading experience.

Regulatory tensions loom as adoption accelerates

The expansion comes as prediction markets face increasing scrutiny, particularly in the United States. Regulators have raised concerns over whether event-based contracts resemble unlicensed gambling products, with ongoing disputes involving federal and state authorities.

The Commodity Futures Trading Commission has asserted jurisdiction over the sector, while platforms like Kalshi have drawn political attention through high-profile affiliations and regulatory challenges. These tensions underscore the uncertain legal environment surrounding the industry’s rapid growth.

Nevada judge extends Kalshi ban, calls sports contracts 'indistinguishable' from gambling. Nevada is now the only state with a court-enforced, in-effect ban against the New York-based company.

Via @TheBlockCohttps://t.co/hfLZkUB79w

— Daniel Wallach (@WALLACHLEGAL) April 5, 2026

Against this backdrop, Binance’s global footprint may provide a strategic advantage, allowing it to scale outside of the most restrictive jurisdictions while the regulatory landscape continues to evolve.

Binance’s entry signals a broader shift as major exchanges move to capture a share of the fast-growing prediction market economy. Once considered a niche segment, event-based trading is increasingly being viewed as a core primitive for information markets and price discovery.

By combining zero-fee access, seamless integration, and its existing infrastructure, Binance is positioning itself not just as a participant but as a potential gatekeeper for mainstream adoption. The strategy suggests a future where prediction markets are no longer standalone platforms, but embedded features within larger financial ecosystems.

As competition intensifies, the race may ultimately hinge on who can scale fastest. With its decision to eliminate fees entirely, Binance is making a clear bet: that in the next phase of the market, distribution and accessibility—not pricing—will determine the winner.

data.title: OpenAI Plans Controlled Cybersecurity AI Rollout, Keeping Access Limited to Approved Partners in Defensive Security Sector
data.description:
OpenAI Quietly Builds Restricted Cybersecurity AI As Industry Locks Down Access

OpenAI is moving deeper into cybersecurity, but not in a way the public will easily see.

A new report suggests the company is preparing an advanced AI model designed for cyber operations, with access limited to a small circle of vetted organisations rather than a broad release.

Trusted Access Programme Signals Shift To Controlled AI Rollouts

The model is expected to sit within OpenAI’s “Trusted Access for Cyber” programme, first introduced in February.

The initiative was designed to keep powerful systems out of general circulation and place them directly in the hands of defensive security teams.

Participants are supported with $10 million in API credits, alongside access to tools such as GPT-5.3-Codex, currently OpenAI’s most capable cybersecurity model.

OpenAI launches Trusted Access for Cyber — identity-verified framework giving security pros access to frontier models like GPT-5.3-Codex for vulnerability testing. Balances defensive use vs misuse. https://t.co/H5eN6fMqNs

— The AI Scope (@the_ai_scope) February 28, 2026

While details remain limited, the restricted rollout reflects a deliberate decision to prioritise control over scale, particularly as AI systems grow more capable of identifying and exploiting vulnerabilities.

Anthropic’s Mythos Forces Industry To Rethink Openness

The timing is not coincidental.

Earlier this week, Anthropic revealed its Claude Mythos Preview model, describing it as capable of discovering “tens of thousands of vulnerabilities” across major operating systems and browsers.

The model reportedly identified zero-day flaws with a level of autonomy comparable to experienced human researchers, raising immediate concerns about misuse.

This is big... Anthropic just announced a model so powerful they won't release it to the public out of fear over the damage it will cause 😨

Claude Mythos Preview found thousands of zero-day exploits in every major operating system and web browser...

The numbers are hard to… https://t.co/pEuokoHMA1pic.twitter.com/FlQgGiavsd

— Josh Kale (@JoshKale) April 7, 2026

Anthropic responded by limiting access through its Project Glasswing programme, distributing the model only to a tightly selected group of companies including major cloud providers, chipmakers, and cybersecurity firms.

More than 40 organisations tied to critical infrastructure were granted controlled access under strict conditions.

We’ve partnered with Amazon Web Services, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorganChase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks.

Together we’ll use Mythos Preview to help find and fix flaws in the systems on which the world depends. pic.twitter.com/FnnhSkPLNQ

— Anthropic (@AnthropicAI) April 7, 2026

How Powerful Is Too Powerful For Public Release

Internal testing results added to the unease.

Mythos Preview uncovered previously unknown flaws in widely used software, including a 27-year-old vulnerability in c and an issue in FFmpeg that had escaped millions of prior automated checks.

this new anthropic model is no joke,

i was curious how claude mythos actually works so i dug into it's system DNA, test results... the numbers are just insane:

- it found a 27-year old hidden exploit for $50 in a few hours. elite human researchers would've taken weeks

- 29%… https://t.co/6nAgURv6cApic.twitter.com/j2nA9MxKtF

— Ejaaz (@cryptopunk7213) April 8, 2026

Anthropic stressed that the model was not specifically trained for security tasks, meaning its capabilities emerged from broader improvements in reasoning and code understanding.

That dual-use nature, the ability to both fix and exploit weaknesses, has become a central concern across the industry.

Security Benchmarks Struggle To Keep Up With AI Progress

The rapid leap in capability is also exposing limits in existing safety frameworks.

Anthropic acknowledged that Cybench, a benchmark used to assess cyber risk in AI systems, is “no longer sufficiently informative of current frontier model capabilities”.

The company added that safety evaluations now involve “judgment calls” and carry “more fundamental uncertainty,” signalling that established measurement tools are falling behind the technology they are meant to assess.

Regulators Increase Pressure As Risks Expand

Government scrutiny is rising alongside these developments.

Federal agencies have intensified their focus on AI safety protocols since early April, while Anthropic is already facing pressure after the Pentagon reportedly flagged it as a supply chain risk over restrictions tied to surveillance and weapons-related use cases.

LATEST🇺🇸: Pentagon tags Anthropic as a "National security supply chain risk", and the court just refused to pause it.

Anthropic will challenge this on May 19

This tag can make it harder for Anthropic to work with the US govt or defence deals. pic.twitter.com/69l6pZrf2f

— Bitinning (@bitinning) April 9, 2026

Security experts and former officials have also warned that sufficiently advanced AI systems could be used to disrupt essential infrastructure, including power grids, water systems, and financial networks.

AI Cyber Tools Begin To Resemble Classified Technology

Against this backdrop, OpenAI’s decision to restrict access appears as much about positioning as precaution.

By limiting distribution early, the company signals alignment with regulators and distances itself from the risks of uncontrolled deployment.

At the same time, it reflects a broader shift in how frontier AI is being released.

Instead of public launches, the most capable systems are increasingly handled like sensitive assets — shared selectively, governed by agreements, and reserved for organisations judged capable of managing their risks.

data.title: In the first quarter of 2026, the AI ​​Agent completed its coming-of-age ceremony.
data.description:
Author: Boyang, Tencent Technology

On March 6, 2026, nearly a thousand people lined up outside the Tencent Building in Shenzhen, not to grab a phone, but to ask someone to install software for them. Its scalper price once reached 1000 yuan. Longgang District and Wuxi High-tech Zone directly included this software in their government subsidy documents. Sam Altman admitted that when faced with similar self-driving products, his initial decision not to let AI completely control the computer "only lasted two hours."

This software is called OpenClaw, an open-source AI Agent.

In the first quarter of 2026, it and four other completely different Agent product forms emerged simultaneously within the same window of opportunity.

... OpenClaw focuses on personal assistants, Cowork on office collaboration, Codex App on long-term engineering tasks, Perplexity Computer on unified workstations, and Tencent Cloud ADP on enterprise platforms. These five companies each pursuing their own paths is no coincidence. A coincidence would be one company just happen to have created a good product. The fact that all five are taking action simultaneously can only mean one thing—a certain underlying condition has just matured, and everyone has sensed its potential. This quarter, our focus is not on isolated events, but on structural changes—those things that truly change the game. There are four selection criteria. First, industry-wide resonance. It's not a single company's isolated action, but multiple companies simultaneously moving in the same direction. Five companies launched Agent products simultaneously, dozens of teams were building constraint frameworks simultaneously, and at least three independent routes successfully completed recursive development. When everyone acts simultaneously, it's not a matter of who has the foresight; the foundation has changed. Second, causal correlation. These aren't four events that happened to coincide in the same quarter, but rather the preceding events directly spawned the following ones; removing any link would render the latter invalid. Third, a qualitative change is perceived. This indicates that these trends are not minor advancements within the industry, but rather have crossed a critical point, significantly elevating to a level that the general public can perceive. The long queues in Shenzhen to install OpenClaw made social news, the "lobster war" became a public topic, the government included agents in subsidy documents, and 22% of employees were secretly using them without the IT department's knowledge. When a technological trend overflows the tech circle and enters public discussion, it is no longer just an "industry trend," but a signal of a turning point in the times. Fourth, cognition is irreversible. Specific products will be replaced, specific frameworks will be iterated, but the ideas behind these trends will not disappear. For example, the consensus that "Agents need discipline" will not regress, the direction that "experience should be reusable by Agents" will not regress, and the expectation that "Agents should be able to improve themselves" will not regress. Forms will change, but cognition will not. Four of these four points apply to Q1. 1. Automated AI Agents are entering productization. Agents can finally work independently, moving from minute-level demonstrations to day-level execution. 2. **Constraint Engineering:** Agents learned to follow rules, and within six weeks, the industry forced the development of a complete disciplinary framework. 3. **Recursive Development:** Agents began to grow independently, not just by executing tasks, but by improving the way they executed them. 4. **Skill Ecosystem:** Agents began to inherit the experience of their predecessors through the Skill model, and for the first time, human industry know-how had a format that could be directly reused by agents. Moreover, these four forces are not parallel, but rather form a flywheel. After agents could work independently, they exposed their tendency to disregard rules, forcing the development of constraint engineering; constraint engineering provided discipline, enabling recursive development to run; recursive development created a strong need for the reuse of experience, giving rise to the skill ecosystem; the skill ecosystem, in turn, allowed agents to handle more complex tasks, and the flywheel began its next cycle. Q1 is the first quarter in which this flywheel completes its rotation. On April 10, 2026, Tencent News released the "AI Trend Research White Paper 2026Q1" (hereinafter referred to as the "White Paper"). This 59-page report focuses on the operating logic of the entire flywheel. This article is a condensed version of the White Paper, offering 25 specific judgments based on these four forces. The productization of long-range agents is their coming-of-age ceremony. In the past, agents were like children showcasing their talents. Asking them to perform a piece was amazing, but you wouldn't dare entrust them with anything. Previously, model demonstrations would amaze everyone after three steps, but by the fifth step, they would completely lose their global perspective and start acting erratically. This has changed. The change isn't simply that the model's intelligence is higher; it's that the agent can finally achieve "you go to sleep, and it does the work on its own." The Cursor Agent has reached a single-task runtime of 36 hours. Claude Code has submitted up to 4% of the world's publicly available GitHub code in a single day, generating approximately $2.5 billion in annualized revenue. Dario Amodei confirmed that over 90% of Claude's new code was written by the AI ​​itself. An engineering lead within Anthropic even said, "I no longer write any code; I just let Opus do it, and I edit it." Anthropic released 74 updates in 52 days. Codex's weekly active users surpassed 1.6 million, and its desktop application downloads exceeded 1 million. Among them, OpenClaw, the most dazzling, saw its GitHub stars surge from 9,000 to 247,000 in 60 days, reaching 2 million monthly active users. Furthermore, Karpathy called Moltbook, which drove 1.5 million agent registrations, "the closest thing to science fiction taking flight in recent times." OpenAI announced its acquisition of OpenClaw's founder on Valentine's Day. The reaction in China was even more intense. At least nine companies launched desktop agent products in the same quarter: Tencent bundled them with WeChat and Enterprise WeChat, ByteDance anchored Lark and cloud SaaS, Alibaba entered the general office market with coding tools, and Baidu lowered the barrier to entry with search skills. The industry calls this the "Lobster Wars"—named after OpenClaw's logo, a lobster, symbolizing that agents have finally grown claws to grab things. The agent can indeed work independently now. But why now of all times? 1. Breaking out of the niche isn't about capability, it's about accessibility. OpenClaw's six dimensions—persistent online presence, heartbeat mechanism, externalized memory, Skills, browser takeover, and remote node invocation—are not original. AutoGPT and various browser proxies had already touted this. But OpenClaw welded them together, creating a qualitative leap. What truly propelled it to mainstream success were two more fundamental elements: IM (Instant Messaging) access and 24/7 proactiveness. Cowork's capabilities almost entirely rivaled, and even surpassed, OpenClaw. Anthropic's three-tiered product system—Claude Code command line, Cowork desktop application, and Computer Use + Dispatch cross-device remote control—was far more sophisticated than OpenClaw in terms of technical depth. Computer Use matched human performance on the OSWorld benchmark (72.5% vs. 72.4% human). But it lacked two things. IM allows the agent to wait for you in your most familiar interface. 24/7 operation allows it to wake up and patrol automatically without waiting for you to speak. Combined, the agent no longer waits for you to speak; it proactively seeks you out. OpenClaw doesn't explain context windows or search enhancements to users at all, simply stating, "I will always be online, I will remember what you say, and I will get things done automatically." Seeing the results first and explaining the principles later, this down-to-earth approach directly breaks down technical barriers. 22% of employees secretly started using OpenClaw without the IT department's knowledge. Accessibility outweighs capability. While OpenClaw's technical depth didn't capture users' hearts like Cowork's, it did so because it appeared before users with the right interface, at the right time, and in the right manner. 2. Five Branches Appear Simultaneously; OpenClaw Isn't the Only One. Among automation products released around the same time as OpenClaw, we can see five routes. Behind them are two conditions fulfilled simultaneously. First, the model finally crossed the passing mark of "sustainable execution." The current model still makes mistakes, but at least it can hold on for dozens of steps in a loop, without suddenly forgetting what it's doing halfway through. This difference is decisive—local errors can be corrected with system scaffolding, but global crashes are incurable. Secondly, the Harness (scaffolding) engineering methodology is robust enough. Memory has changed from a black-box vector database to plain text files that users can directly browse and edit, supporting Git version control. The execution environment now includes gateways, heartbeat mechanisms, browser takeover, and remote node calls. With capabilities and scaffolding in place simultaneously, long-range agents have become the industry's common choice. Codex App's Worktree architecture allows multiple agents to work in parallel on the same code repository; five parallel Worktrees reduced a 42-minute task to 14 minutes, with zero merge conflicts. Agent execution spans have officially moved from minutes to days. 3. After coding, the second essential scenario has emerged. OpenClaw, coupled with the Skill marketplace, transforms the Agent from a developer tool into a general-purpose work assistant, capable of handling everything from research and monitoring to content generation and customer service. The 13,700 Skills cover scenarios far exceeding coding. The pattern here is clear: as long as a Skill standardizes domain know-how (industry experience), any long-term, high-cognitive task is within the Agent's capabilities. However, this isn't the case for short-term, low-cognitive operations—ordering a cup of milk tea might take you 30 seconds on your phone, but an Agent would be slower. 4. Walled gardens can't stop Agents. In China, nine major companies are each vying for entry points with their own IM apps. This battle revolves around "which app the Agent should reside in," a fight for ecosystem entry rights. But by the end of Q1, this wall began to loosen. QClaw supports Lark and DingTalk, and OpenClaw-CN has built-in compatibility with five major IM platforms. This is because when an Agent needs to handle customer messages in WeChat, team collaboration in Lark, and approval processes in DingTalk simultaneously, it can't exist solely within the confines of one IM platform. The more essential the Agent's needs, the greater the cross-platform pressure, and the less able the walled garden will be. Now it's about "who is closest to the user," but in the future, it's about "who can make the Agent work seamlessly everywhere." Silicon Valley and China have very different approaches. Silicon Valley is fighting a battle between "model providers vs. middleware"—Google suddenly and massively banned users calling Gemini through OpenClaw in mid-February without any prior warning, shutting down hundreds of paid accounts overnight. The stated reason was "malicious use leading to a computational load far exceeding expectations," but in reality, OpenClaw's heartbeat mechanism performs a full context check every 30 minutes with tens of thousands of tokens. The actual cost to a single Ultra subscriber, converted to API prices, could reach $1000-$3600, far exceeding the $250 monthly fee. This directly impacts the subscription-based business model. Anthropic directly characterized this behavior as "token arbitrage," requiring users to access the platform via API keys (at 5-10 times the subscription price), and ultimately blocking OpenClaw's subscription entry point in early April. OpenAI, on the other hand, chose the opposite path, acquiring OpenClaw's founder and then whitelisting it. In short, when an open-source middleware allows users to bypass official pricing to access model capabilities, platforms must choose between blocking and incorporating it. The same OpenClaw sparked a pricing debate in Silicon Valley and an entry war in China. 5. The first wave of substitution landed on outsourcing services. Julien Bek of Sequoia Capital calculated that for every $1 a company spends on software, it spends $6 on services. Accounting, legal services, IT hosting, recruitment, insurance brokerage—all are services. Agent pricing is shifting from seat (per person) and feature (per function) to workflow (per process) and outcome (per result), but the real breakthrough isn't replacing people, but replacing outsourcing contracts. Think about it: if a task is already outsourced, it means the company already accepts external execution, has an existing budget, and is buying results. Replacing outsourcing is like changing suppliers; replacing internal staff is like organizational restructuring. The former faces an order of magnitude less resistance. This explains why vertical autopilots like Harvey (legal), Anterior (medical approval), and WithCoverage (insurance) scale up much faster than general-purpose agents—they're not targeting the political minefield of "AI replacing humans," but rather the natural commercial area of ​​"AI replacing outsourcing." This is also evident in OpenClaw; the first tasks individual users had agents perform were precisely the same tasks previously done by paid virtual assistants, such as monitoring, research, and social media management. The service market, six times the size of the software market, is the true foundation of autopilot. 6. Technological capabilities have outpaced organizational interfaces. Block (the parent company of Square + Cash App) demonstrates an extreme example: the company was directly restructured into a four-layer intelligent agent architecture, eliminating middle management, and product roadmaps were automatically generated by failure signals from the intelligent layer, eliminating product managers making decisions about features. However, the prerequisite for Block is the high-frequency structured data of a bilateral trading platform, which most companies lack. What truly hinders an agent from transitioning from copilot to autopilot is not model capability, but rather three organizational interfaces, each with its own independent pace: the evaluation system (how to determine if an agent is performing adequately; accounting has GAAP standards, but legal advice and recruitment screening criteria are far from mature), authorization boundaries (who authorizes its execution, and whether these boundaries are written in the system prompt or the business rules system), and liability attribution (who takes responsibility for mistakes; in copilot mode, someone signs off, while in autopilot mode, the system lacks both professional qualifications and professional insurance). Model capability is a function of computing power and capital; it can increase with money. These three factors, however, are functions of time and regulations, and won't automatically improve simply because the model becomes stronger. Between copilot and autopilot, there is likely a severely underestimated transitional form—AI executing autonomously within clearly defined boundaries, defined by business rules rather than prompts, automatically escalating to humans upon encountering anomalies. This is closer to monetization and more valuable than pure autopilot. The ability to act independently is the starting point of the flywheel. However, the agent immediately exposed fatal flaws. OpenClaw's 512 security vulnerabilities, 341 malicious skills, and bills often reaching hundreds or thousands of dollars are obvious pitfalls. Independent capability brings new problems, and these new problems force the flywheel's second driving force: constraint engineering, teaching the agent to follow rules. The number one problem exposed after the agent can act independently is its failure to follow rules. Memory is like a goldfish; it takes three steps and declares itself a success, giving itself a high score, but it doesn't run end-to-end at all. Q1 forced out a solution in just 15 weeks. From Anthropic publishing the first Harness blog post on December 5, 2025, to LangChain's generalized definition on March 10, Harness Engineering achieved industry consensus. This speed itself shows how impatient everyone was. Because the Agent was already on the road, but the rules hadn't been established yet. 1. From "What to See" to "How to Continuously Do It Right" Context Engineering manages the information layer, that is, "What should the model see?" Harness Engineering manages the structural layer, that is, "how the model consistently does things right over dozens of rounds." The latter is the true paradigm shift in Q1. Claude Code generates 135,000 to 326,000 public commits daily on GitHub, accounting for 4% of global public commits, and is projected to reach 20% by the end of the year. With the agent already running so deep in the code repository, without a dedicated set of disciplinary constraints, problems are bound to arise sooner or later. At the Shanghai Summit on March 27th, Tencent Senior Executive Vice President Tang Daosheng set the tone for the Chinese version: "AI implementation is not just an algorithm problem, but also an engineering problem. As the capability gap between mainstream large models narrows, the core of enterprise competition is no longer the strength of the model itself, but the ability to leverage the value of the model through engineering methods." Other major companies have also entered the fray. ByteDance's DeerFlow 2.0 directly uses "Super Agent Harness" in its GitHub description, which is likely the first time a Chinese open-source project has used this term in its product positioning. It skyrocketed from 22K to 52K stars on GitHub within a month, demonstrating the overwhelming desire for Harness. 2. Harness is a Three-Layer Shell To explain Harness clearly, we can think of the Agent as a car. The model is the engine, and the Prompt is the steering wheel. But an engine and steering wheel alone don't make a car; you also need a transmission, dashboard, and brakes. Harness is those components, and in current engineering practice, there are three layers. The first layer is process control, specifically designed to combat disobedience. For example, some drivers forget things like goldfish, declare completion after only three steps, and are completely unaware of bugs in their environment. The solution is externalizing state (AGENTS.md / progress file), task splitting, and forcing adherence to steps. The second layer is concurrent scheduling, specifically designed to combat slacking off. With 100 agents running simultaneously, it's easy for everyone to avoid risks and only do the simplest minor modifications, leaving the real challenges untouched. The solution is a multi-agent hierarchical structure, role separation (Planner planning → Generator generation → Evaluator evaluation), and mechanisms to prevent slacking off. The third layer is verification and error correction, specifically designed to combat misplaced confidence. Giving oneself high marks is what Anthropic calls "self-deception." The solution is independent Evaluators, sandbox isolation, and Git transaction boundaries (Branch is the sandbox, PR is approval, and only merge is a commit). CLI, Skills, and externalized memory formats, which are frequently mentioned and became development trends in the first quarter, aren't entirely Harness in engineering practice; they're Agentic Infrastructure. Harness only cares about "how the car runs stably," while Infrastructure manages the "road conditions and gas stations" that enable speed and convenience.

3. Each layer of the shell was forced out by bugs

Harness didn't appear out of thin air, but rather stemmed from a series of specific problems encountered in engineering practice. When people wanted the model to perform longer-term tasks, bugs forced it out time and again.

The origin of the first layer. Users dump large requirements on the Agent all at once, and it tries to complete them all at once, only to crash at step 30. Anthropic's solution is simple, like a relay race—the "initialization Agent" sets up the environment, writes a handover list (claude-progress.txt), and then leaves. The "coding Agent" reads the handover list each time it enters, understands where the previous leg is, performs only one function, updates the list after completion, and then leaves. The key point is that agents do not share dialogue history, but only transmit information through files. This is because the dialogue history is completely drowned out by the noise of the previous nine rounds by the tenth round.

The Origin of the Second Layer. Cursor discovered that Agents in a flat structure were extremely risk-averse, preferring to make meaningless minor modifications rather than tackle difficult problems, resulting in the entire system running idle. Anthropic introduced a "Client-Supplier" architecture—Planners write specifications, Generators implement each feature according to the specifications, and a Sprint contract is written before starting work on each feature. Evaluators test features using a real browser (not looking at code), scoring them based on four dimensions: product depth, functionality, visual design, and code quality. Failure to meet standards results in a sprint failure and rework. An interesting finding was that it was much easier to make the "evaluator" stricter than to teach the "coder" to self-criticize. The Origin of the Third Layer. Agents run their own tests and claim "no bugs," but end-to-end execution is completely unsuccessful. Anthropic calls this "self-deception"—similar to having students grade their own essays; the score will never be low. There must be an independent evaluator and sandbox isolation. Mitchell Hashimoto's AGENTS.md in the open-source project Ghostty isn't a design document at all; it's an incident log—if an agent modifies a file it shouldn't, add a rule "Don't modify the vendor/ directory." If an agent uses an outdated interface, add a rule "Use the v2 API instead of v1." If an agent writes incorrect information in its commit message, add a formatting guideline. He found that the background agent only ran effectively 10-20% of the time on normal workdays, initially "taking longer than doing it manually." But once the inflection point was passed, and the rules accumulated to a certain density, the agent's error rate significantly decreased. OpenAI discovered a more chronic problem: without a dedicated maintenance mechanism, repositories used by agents would noticeably deteriorate after about 2-3 months. It's like having ten interns come in every day to work, leaving behind a bunch of "temporary solutions"—after three months, no one can distinguish which code was written seriously and which was just makeshift. They built three mechanisms: architectural constraint declarations (explicitly stating the framework, pattern, and naming conventions used in the AGENTS.md file), Doc gardening (regularly cleaning up expired comments and redundant documentation left by agents), and Anti-slop routines (anti-degradation checks, cleaning up inconsistencies and duplicate code accumulated by agents). It's not a one-time fix; it needs to be continuously implemented. 4. Reskinning is more cost-effective than remodeling, but not cheap. LangChain conducted an experiment: with the same model, replacing it with a Harness skin increased the Terminal Bench 2.0 pass rate from 52.8% to 66.5%, without changing the weight by a single byte, and the ranking jumped from outside the top 30 to the top 5. This is the effect of Harness. However, this effect is expensive. Anthropic's cost data shows that running the Solo Agent bare-metal version of the same 2D game costs only $9 and takes 20 minutes, but the resulting product has major functionalities and is unplayable. Applying the complete Harness skin costs $200 and takes 6 hours, resulting in a fully functional, visually excellent, and playable product. The 20x cost doesn't result in "a little better," but rather a life-or-death difference between "usable" and "unusable." From this perspective, Harness is currently the most cost-effective capability amplifier. However, it's not cheap at all; it's not in the same league as maintaining a continuously running, well-behaved agent or a chat assistant that only asks questions occasionally. Bills of hundreds or thousands of dollars often deter many users after just a few weeks. Cost remains the biggest obstacle to widespread adoption. 5. Harness is a temporary moat, but the compensation is shifting. While the entire industry is frantically building Harness, Anthropic is already dismantling the shell it has built. After the release of Opus 4.6, they removed Context Reset because the model's context management capabilities were already strong enough that context reset was no longer necessary. They also removed Sprint Contract because the new model could control its own pace, eliminating the need to sign an acceptance contract before each round of work. The Evaluator was also changed from adversarial in each round to QA in the last round. In Anthropic's own words, "Every component of Harness is coded with an assumption about what the model cannot do. When the assumption no longer holds, the component should be removed." The fact that they could remove these components shows that the initial setup was effective. The decisive removal shows that they have always been clear about what they are compensating for. The difficulty is not in removing components, but in judging when to remove them. Removing them too early means the model can't handle it, and the system will collapse; removing them too late means the shell obscures the model's true capabilities. When a model evolves, you might think the shell is helping, but it's actually getting in the way. The path to simplicity requires navigating through complexity. However, currently only Anthropic has completed the full cycle from "addition" to "deconstruction." 6. There's an even bigger shell beyond Harness. The five points of consensus discussed in Q1—Markdown as a state carrier, Git for transaction boundaries, the CLI renaissance (Agents need a structured text interface; a single `git diff --stat` command provides an overview of all changes), real-time testing (Agents modify a file and immediately run tests; test results become instant feedback signals for each step), and Skill for knowledge encapsulation—don't all belong to Harness. The larger framework is called Agentic Infrastructure, which consists of five layers: Context layer (what the agent can remember), Tool interface layer (what the agent can do), Harness layer (how the agent is managed), Knowledge layer (how the agent knows how to do things), and Economics layer (how much it costs to run the agent). Harness is the most crucial layer, but it's far from the whole story. Currently, the industry's attention is focused on the Harness layer because it directly determines whether an agent can be used. But the next phase of the battle may unfold at other layers—the memory quality of the Context layer (memory retention across days and sessions is still unreliable), the execution environment resources of the tool interface layer (Anthropic found that simply relaxing resource restrictions can improve the success rate by 6 percentage points), the Skill triggering mechanism of the knowledge layer (Vercel's evaluation shows that in 56% of cases, the Agent doesn't actively look up its own Skills), and cost control of the economic layer (model routing, budget allocation, and parallelization strategies are still basically decided on a whim). There's also a problem everyone knows but no one has a good answer to—organizational governance. "Who approved the Agent to write this code?" In most companies, there's no standard answer. Audit logs, decision tracing, and code ownership are crucial. A 3-person startup team might manage with workflow-level approval processes. But in a 500-person company, without an independent governance framework, relying solely on technical means is simply not enough. This is the less responsible, but crucial part of the Harness architecture—the Agent that truly gives people peace of mind. 7. Agent Failures Can Finally Be Diagnosed The three-layer shell + five-layer Infra framework provides the industry's first diagnostic framework. Agent crashes are no longer simply a matter of "the model isn't good enough." Is it due to poor process control? Uncontrolled concurrency scheduling? Missing verification steps? Insufficient execution environment resources, untriggered skills, or simply uneconomical costs? Previously, all these were vague issues attributed to "insufficient model capabilities," but now there are precise engineering attributions. "Why did the Agent crash?" has transformed from a vague complaint into a locatable and fixable engineering problem. Engineering of constraints gives the Agent discipline, allowing the flywheel to start its next cycle. A disciplined Agent finally possesses a previously impossible ability: to continuously improve itself in long-term cycles, instead of collapsing at the tenth step. This is the premise of the flywheel's third force. Recursive development allows the Agent to become stronger. The previous two chapters discussed how the Agent, as a product and system, establishes itself. This chapter discusses in which scenario, once the Agent has discipline, it first breaks through the role of "executor" and begins to improve its execution methods. The answer is development. Because development is inherently verifiable (a successful test means a successful test), reversible (Git allows one-click undo), and readable and writable (the code itself is plain text that machines can directly manipulate). Once these three conditions are met, the Agent can enter a complete loop of "execution → verification → problem discovery → modification → re-execution". There are three paths here, each with impressive results. AlphaEvolve recovers 0.7% of global computing power, Minimax M2.7 showed a 30% improvement in internal evaluations after 100+ rounds of autonomous iteration, and Karpathy's Autoresearch runs 50 experiments in one night. These three open-source practices demonstrate that recursive R&D is already generating real value. Large, non-open-source model companies have also acknowledged this in various interviews. 1. Exploration, Optimization, and Engineering Flow are three completely different types of recursion. Now let's take a closer look at these three methods. Exploratory, AlphaEvolve. It doesn't tune parameters; it searches for new algorithms that humans have never seen before. It's an evolutionary system composed of Gemini Flash (responsible for breadth, quickly generating a large number of variants) and Gemini Pro (responsible for depth, refining the optimal solution). The entire process produces human-readable code, not a black box—understandable, debuggable, and directly deployable. Its discovered data center scheduling algorithm has been running in Google's production environment for a year, continuously recovering 0.7% of global computing power, equivalent to billions of dollars. It provided optimization solutions for key TPU circuitry, increasing the speed of a key computing core in the Gemini architecture by 23% and optimizing the underlying instructions of FlashAttention by 32.5%. It improved 20% of the known optimal solutions to over 50 open mathematical problems, including improvements to Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm proposed in 1969. The value of this recursion is that it can potentially change the direction of a discipline. (Optimization-oriented, Autoresearch, and M2.7. The objective function is known; it involves iteratively approximating the optimal solution.) Karpathy distilled the core loop to its ultimate form with 630 lines of Python code—three files (train.py can be modified / prepare.py cannot be changed / program.md contains instructions written by humans for the Agent), plus a "ratchet" rule (only retaining results better than the last, never regressing). It runs approximately 12 experiments per hour, 80-100 per night. It garnered 23K GitHub stars in three days and rose to 35K in three weeks. This model has been applied beyond machine learning—LangChain founder Harrison Chase used the same three-file architecture to optimize the LangChain Agent itself, and it's also used for database query optimization and customer service ticket routing. Any optimization problem with quantifiable good or bad performance can be applied. M2.7 goes a step further. MiniMax allows the model to act as a "research agent," responsible for improving its own reinforcement learning training process, autonomously building dozens of complex skills, updating the memory system, and continuously optimizing the entire Harness architecture. Its memory is structured in three layers: after each iteration, it writes a "short-term note" (what was done and what the results were), and simultaneously conducts a "self-criticism" (what went wrong and how to improve next time). Before each new iteration, it reviews the entire history before deciding on its direction. This is similar to a researcher writing an experimental log and reflecting daily, rather than starting from scratch every day. After 100+ rounds of autonomous iteration, internal evaluation improved by 30%, and its SWE-Pro score of 56.22% matched GPT-5.3-Codex. In 22 ML competitions, after three rounds of self-optimization, it won 9 gold, 5 silver, and 1 bronze medals. The API price is only 8% of Claude 4.5 Sonnet. The value of this recursion lies in accelerating existing pathways. (Engineering manifold: Codex participated in OpenAI's internal R&D, and Claude wrote Anthropic's own code. Dario Amodei confirmed that over 90% of the new code was written by the AI ​​itself.) The value of this recursion is the most basic: freeing up manpower and accelerating iteration. The three closed-loop properties are completely different, but the Agent is no longer just "doing the work"; it's improving its work methods. 2. The speed of the human brain becomes the system's speed limiter. In Autoresearch, humans have completely withdrawn from the execution phase; they only need to set up three files, and the Agent can run itself. However, the entire process still requires human intervention. Two things AI cannot automate are defining the goal ("what metrics to optimize") and judging boundaries ("what directions to avoid"). When the agent runs 50 rounds a night and 500 rounds a day, the speed at which humans can automatically set goals simply cannot keep up. The bottleneck of self-evolution, which still requires human intervention, shifts from "human hands not being fast enough" to "human brains not being fast enough." Yang Zhilin of Moon's Dark Side stated at the Zhongguancun Forum, "AI will define the most suitable reward function for that environment, and even explore new network architectures." Luo Fuli of Xiaomi stated, "By adding a verifiable constraint and a loop to the existing agent framework, we can prevent the model from stopping." "The team is already using this method for research tasks, and the efficiency has increased nearly tenfold." She shortened her estimate of the time for AI to fully evolve from "three to five years" to "one to two years." Both individuals are attempting to break through the same bottleneck. The ultimate question is who holds the agenda-setting power. Autoresearch is a "faster experimental assistant"—humans set the goals, and the agent runs. "Self-evolution" means AI decides its own research agenda, sets its own goals, designs experiments, runs them, evaluates them, and adjusts its direction. The difference lies not in technical capabilities, but in who steers the ship. 3. The acceleration of recursive R&D is exponential, not linear. Mimimax M2.7 optimizes its toolchain using its own output. The optimized toolchain makes the next round more efficient, and the more efficient next round produces an even better toolchain. This illustrates that the R&D speed brought about by AI self-evolution is compound interest, not linear. However, compound interest has a fatal prerequisite: each round of improvement must be a "genuine improvement," not something artificially created. If the evaluation pipeline itself is biased, compound interest becomes "running faster and faster in the wrong direction." This is the same problem as Goodhart's Law: when a metric becomes the goal, it is no longer a good metric. The scheduling algorithm discovered by AlphaEvolve took a full year to prove its effectiveness in production environments, but most teams can't wait a year. There is a structural mismatch between short-term evaluation metrics and long-term real value. The authenticity of the evaluation pipeline determines whether compound interest can be sustained. 4. The next battleground is self-evolving infrastructure. For the past decade, the competition was about training infrastructure—who had the largest computing clusters, the fastest data pipelines, and the most stable training frameworks. The next battleground has changed; it's called self-evolving infrastructure. Based on current practices, it mainly comprises five components: separation of mutable assets and immutable infrastructure (clearly defining what the agent can and cannot modify), evaluation pipeline (quickly and accurately judging "this round is better than the last"), memory and selection mechanism (remembering good experiences and discarding bad ones), execution environment (sandbox isolation and ample resources; Anthropic found that simply relaxing resource restrictions improved performance by 6 percentage points), and dynamic tools and skills (the M2.7 agent created dozens of auxiliary tools for itself to run reinforcement learning experiments). AlphaEvolve can reclaim 0.7% of global computing power not only because of the strength of the Gemini model, but also because Google has the world's best evaluation pool and parallel execution environment. When model capabilities begin to converge, the difference in self-evolving infrastructure is where the real gap widens. The agent has learned to grow on its own, and the flywheel has completed its third revolution. However, recursive development exposed a new bottleneck—the agent accumulates experience from scratch in each loop, while decades of industry know-how are simply unusable. If there were a format that allowed the agent to directly inherit previous experience, the starting point of recursion would no longer be zero, but rather the endpoint of the previous iterations. Q1 happens to offer such a format. Skill ecosystem, when knowledge is no longer attached to humans. Opus 4.6 can write code in any language. But it doesn't know your team's coding standards, your industry's approval processes, or where your project's technical debt lies. "This API has a hidden rate limit in high-concurrency scenarios." "The migration tool in this framework had a bug before v3.2; you had to manually change a configuration first." "Our team never uses ORM's cascade delete because of a major incident three years ago." These are all know-how pieces gained by senior engineers through trial and error; they're not in the training data and aren't suitable for hardcoding into product logic. Q1, for the first time, these experiences have a format that can be packaged, distributed, and infinitely reused. It's called a Skill. 1. A Skill fills the gaps in experience, not in technology. A Skill is neither a document nor code, but a structured knowledge package containing triggering conditions (what scenarios to use it in), standard operating procedures (how to do it step by step), executable scripts (tools that can be run directly), and reference materials (background knowledge). What it does is simple: it transforms "what veteran employees have in their heads" into a format that the Agent can read and execute. Prompt addresses "how to explain things more clearly this time," offering immediacy but lacking reusability. Workflow is a deterministic process orchestration, stable but rigid. Skill lies between the two—more stable than Prompt (structured, version controllable), more flexible than Workflow (the model can be flexibly applied based on the current situation), and lighter than retraining a model (modifying a Markdown file vs. retraining a large model with billions of parameters).

It's not that we're smarter, it's that we're more knowledgeable.

2. Write Once, Reuse Infinitely

Previously, the transfer of domain experience relied on mentorship, documentation, and training. This was slow, non-scalable, and heavily dependent on individuals.

Now, a senior engineer can write a TDD Skill in two hours, and thousands of Agent instances across the company can load it simultaneously, instantly mastering it. Domain experience that previously took juniors two years to accumulate can now be packaged into a single file and distributed. Knowledge is no longer attached to individuals, but to the structure itself.

Users of the popular Skill Superpowers framework (143,000+ installs, 93K stars on GitHub) say, "Skills aren't suggestions, they're structured decision trees. They give Claude discipline." "I've become lazy with TDD, now skills remember it for me." Skills don't make agents smarter, they make them more reliable. Looking at the Brainstorming Skill in Superpowers helps you understand the difference between a Skill and ordinary prompts. Agents have a fatal tendency to start writing code immediately upon receiving a vague requirement, only to realize halfway through that they misunderstood and have to start over. The Brainstorming Skill places a hard barrier between the Agent and the code; no code should be written before the design is approved by the user. It defines a 9-step checklist, from exploring the project context to proposing 2-3 solutions (including trade-offs and recommendations), and then to user review. The Prompt provides the "principles" (which the Agent can choose to ignore), while the Skill provides the "access control" (you can't proceed to the next step without passing through). Skills can also be interconnected; after Brainstorming completes the task, it automatically calls writing-plans (writing implementation plans), then executing-plans (execution plans), using test-driven development during execution. This makes Skills not isolated capability packages, but standardized modules that can form complete workflows. 3. Openness and security are mutually exclusive. In terms of ecosystem, three paths are running simultaneously. ClawHub takes a community-open approach, growing extremely rapidly, accumulating over 13,700 Skills in six months, with the highest number of installations for a single Skill reaching 180,000. Popular skills are naturally tiered: the survival tier (Web Browsing, 180,000 installations), the efficiency tier (Telegram Bot, 145,000 installations), and the advanced tier (Capability Evolver, 35,000 installations). In the advanced tier, the agent automatically identifies recurring patterns and creates new skills, essentially giving the AI ​​a "self-evolution" button. ClawHub also achieves cross-platform compatibility, supporting direct import of plugin packages from Claude, Codex, and Cursor platforms, automatic mapping and execution, and interoperability of over 4,000 cross-platform skills, firmly establishing its position as the "universal middleware." However, this openness comes at a cost. According to relevant research, in the investigation of over 1,200 skills, 341 malicious skills were discovered (accounting for 11.3% of the market), and 36% contained keyword injection prompts. VirusTotal directly characterized this as an "AI version of npm poisoning." Even more alarming was the supply chain contamination incident earlier this year, where attackers triggered an AI-powered traffic redirection bot to execute malicious code using only a carefully crafted post title. This code poisoned caches, stole tokens, and ultimately forced backdoors onto the machines of thousands of developers. Within 60 days, the MCP ecosystem revealed 30 CVEs, 82% of which had path traversal vulnerabilities, and 38% lacked any authentication. When Skill calls external tools through MCP, the two layers of risk are amplified. In response, Chinese vendors have placed considerable emphasis on security. Tencent SkillHub uses platform review, ensuring security, but its openness is limited. Kouzi and DeerFlow are open-source and controllable. A Skill is essentially a Markdown file; it's version controlled via Git and loaded on demand without occupying a context window. The Skill format is established; now the competition within the ecosystem is about who distributes it and how. 341 malicious Skills indicate this ecosystem is extremely immature. However, "immature" and "unfounded" are two different things. 4. 56% of Agents don't actively look up Skills. Skills exist, but their integration with the system is far from mature. Vercel conducted a highly precise evaluation, testing with the Next.js 16 API, which wasn't in the model's training data. Without providing any information to the Agent, the pass rate was 53%. Giving it an AGENTS.md index file (directly inserted into the system prompts) caused the pass rate to soar to 100%. Giving it a Skill (putting it on a shelf and letting it search for itself) has a 53% pass rate, which is practically the same as not giving it anything. In 56% of cases, the Agent doesn't even realize it needs to look something up. No matter how many good Skills are available in the market, if the Agent doesn't know how to find them, it's as if they don't exist. DeerFlow's solution is to explicitly load Skills when breaking down tasks at the orchestration layer—not relying on the Agent to search itself, but having the system decide for it during the planning phase. This essentially pushes the problem back to the Harness workflow layer. Before the triggering mechanism matures, the value of Skills will continue to be severely underestimated. 5. Skills don't shake the interface layer, they shake the process layer itself. What is the core value of SaaS? It's solidifying domain workflows into software. A CRM is essentially the software-based "customer management know-how." Skill does the same thing, but at several orders of magnitude lower costs (writing Markdown vs. developing a SaaS system), several orders of magnitude faster iteration (changing a single line vs. releasing a version), and several orders of magnitude faster distribution (direct agent loading vs. user registration + learning + data migration). MCP once challenged SaaS, but it only affected the interface layer; the process itself remained. The business model was challenged but not fundamentally damaged. SFT also faced challenges earlier, but ordinary people couldn't grasp it and couldn't achieve scalable compound interest. Skill is different; it challenges the process layer itself. When a skill allows an agent to complete the entire "managing customers with Salesforce" process, users no longer need the Salesforce interface. The barrier to entry is extremely low (writing Markdown), and compound interest can accumulate (13,700+ in six months). Moreover, as Skills mature, the next threat to SaaS may be Apps. When Agents can complete tasks like "ordering takeout + comparing prices + reaching the minimum spending requirement for discounts" through Skill combinations, will you still need to open Meituan? 6. The App Store for Agents is Born. GPT Store sold chatbots and simple workflows, but it failed because it wasn't a necessity. ClawHub sells "capability packages that enable Agents to do specific tasks," which is a necessity in scenarios where Agents are continuously running. OpenClaw released an epic update at the end of Q1, with 45 new features, 13 disruptive changes, and 82 fixes. Agent timeouts were increased from 10 minutes to 48 hours, a pluggable sandbox backend architecture was added, and three major search services were built-in. The Skill Market, in this context, has become a nascent App Store. Unlike any previous attempt, this one is built on the foundation of a continuously running Agent. 7. Long-Term Skills Will Repeat Harness's Mistakes Short Skills ("indented with 2 spaces") are almost error-free. But long-term Skills (writing a complete marketing plan according to the AIDA model, 2000+ characters, a dozen steps) suffer from Agent skipping steps, not following instructions, and forgetting previous requirements halfway through. These failure patterns are exactly the same as when Harness's first layer was created. M2.7 maintained a 97% single-step compliance rate in 40 complex Skill tests, which sounds good. However, related research shows that 3% of failures accumulate in long-term tasks. Even with 97% success rate at each of the ten steps, the overall success rate drops to only 74%. Skills need their own Harness: step breakdown, progress tracking, intermediate verification, and rollback mechanisms. But currently, no one is doing this. This is a known structural flaw, waiting to be solved by the first wave of people who stumble upon it, just like the three-layered Harness was forced out. 8. After experience is distilled, where do people retreat to? Skills distill human experience into a format that Agents can directly execute. Once a senior engineer writes a Skill, all Agents in the company instantly know it. So what does this engineer do next? In the short term, they "move up" to the judgment and decision-making level. The execution level is handed over to Agents, and humans retreat to defining goals, reviewing quality, and handling edge cases. This is the same issue as the human brain becoming a speed limiter in Chapter 3. But a more acute problem is that an organization might need a thousand executors, but only ten decision-makers. When Skills distill all the know-how at the execution level, the work of a thousand executors is replaced by Agents, who "move up" to the decision-making level, but the decision-making level simply can't accommodate a thousand people. This isn't a job transformation; it's a net reduction in the total workload. Moreover, distillation is irreversible; once experience is written into Skills, the Skills no longer need you. Humans retreat to judgment, but recursive development is taking over judgment (Evaluator). Humans retreat to creation, but AlphaEvolve is discovering algorithms that humans hadn't even thought of. Q1 doesn't answer where humans should retreat to. But it did something even more cruel—it turned this problem from a "philosophical discussion" into a "reality to be faced next quarter."

Epilogue

Four forces, one flywheel.

Productization puts the Agent on the right track, constraint engineering teaches it to follow the rules, recursive development allows it to learn self-growth, and the skill ecosystem allows it to inherit the experience of its predecessors. Each force is the inevitable consequence of the previous force and a necessary prerequisite for the next force.

But the most noteworthy property of the flywheel is not causal progression—it's acceleration.

Skill makes the Agent stronger → the Agent can handle more complex tasks → more complex tasks force more precise constraints → more precise constraints support deeper recursion → deeper recursion produces better Skills. With each rotation, the next rotation is faster. This is not linear growth, it's compound interest.

Productization puts the Agent on the right track, constraint engineering teaches it to follow the rules, recursive development allows it to learn self-growth, and the skill ecosystem allows it to inherit the experience of its predecessors.

Skill makes the Agent stronger → the Agent can handle more complex tasks → more complex tasks force more precise constraints → more precise constraints support deeper recursion → deeper recursion produces better Skill ... Q1 represents the first complete rotation of the flywheel. The speed is still slow, and there's significant friction between the gears. There are 341 malicious skills, a 56% skill trigger failure rate, costs often exceeding thousands of dollars, and a lack of organizational governance. But the flywheel is already turning. The most important aspect of the 25 judgments isn't any single judgment, but the conclusion they all point to: the Agent is no longer a tool requiring human guidance. It's becoming a capable, rule-abiding, evolving, and knowledgeable "new colleague." This new colleague is arriving faster than most people expected, faster than most organizations were prepared, and faster than most discussions about "where to retreat to." Q1 doesn't answer where people should retreat to. But it transformed this issue from a "philosophical discussion" into a "reality to be faced next quarter." The flywheel won't wait for you to figure it out before it starts spinning.

data.title: Is Bitcoin a key factor in the US-Iran ceasefire?
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Earlier this week, the United States and Iran reached a ceasefire agreement with several conditions, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran appears to have agreed to open the waterway, but according to the Financial Times, the country wants to charge a $1 toll for every barrel of oil passing through the waters.

This request itself seems unremarkable. However, the Financial Times also revealed that Iran insists on paying this fee in Bitcoin. If true, this detail could cause a major uproar. This would be the first time hostile nations have publicly agreed to include Bitcoin as a core component of a peace agreement. However, this development is not surprising to the Bitcoin community. For years, we have emphasized that Bitcoin is a digital, decentralized, non-sovereign, and neutral asset that anyone in the world can use. If two countries distrust each other, they can use Bitcoin for economically valuable transactions. After the US imposed severe sanctions on Russia and its oligarchs due to the Russia-Ukraine conflict, the US dollar is no longer suitable for this role. Furthermore, US and multinational corporations are unlikely to choose to use the renminbi, yen, euro, or other fiat currencies for payments. Gold is impractical due to its poor portability and low transaction efficiency. Another alternative is stablecoins, but these assets also face the risk of being frozen, intercepted, or even confiscated. For Iran, which is currently confronting the world's most powerful military force, this risk is clearly too high. This is not the first time Bitcoin has appeared on the geopolitical stage. China's fluctuating policies regarding Bitcoin mining over the years, as well as numerous bilateral provisional trade agreements settled in Bitcoin, have been widely reported. U.S. intelligence agencies have also been using Bitcoin for quite some time. Last year, I had a discussion with Michael Ellis, Deputy Director of the CIA, who explained the agency's views on this digital currency. Regarding the significance of Iran's statement, there are opposing views: some believe that this Gulf state will not actually accept Bitcoin. BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes once astutely wrote: "Unless I see on-chain transactions related to toll fees for ships, I don't believe Iran would use Bitcoin to collect tolls. This is nothing more than the Iranian Revolutionary Guard toying with the dirty Western fiat currency system." However, regardless of whether the Iranian government will actually accept Bitcoin, this claim has gone viral globally, and perhaps this is the most important significance of the whole affair. The era of Bitcoin has arrived. How can we be so sure? Because on the very day Bitcoin was revealed to be involved in the ceasefire peace agreement, Morgan Stanley officially launched its Bitcoin ETF. Morgan Stanley is one of the world's largest financial institutions and one of the oldest investment banks on Wall Street. On the surface, Morgan Stanley seems to have entered the market late, but it has come in with great force. The product's management fee is only 0.14%, the lowest in the entire market. This pricing pressure will inevitably force other market participants to follow suit, and in the long run, it is expected to attract more capital inflows. What I find quite interesting is that the Bitcoin industry is still seeing polarized news. Traditional financial institutions hope that Bitcoin will become a mature and compliant asset so that it can be packaged and sold to clients; while the world's most vicious criminals and terrorists are also using this asset as a last resort to solve their specific problems. People may be reluctant to admit it, but this is precisely what a true product-market fit looks like. Bitcoin appeals to different groups of people in different ways: **If you need to store economic value for a period of time, Bitcoin is an excellent choice; if you need to prevent others from taking that economic value, Bitcoin may be the only choice.** Looking back at the development over the past fifteen years or so, we've come a long way, which is exhilarating. But my intuition tells me that this story is far from over, and the development over the next fifteen years will be even more spectacular.

data.title: From selling his house to buy cryptocurrency to going to jail and publishing a book, CZ's self-defense and self-mythologizing.
data.description: On November 21, 2023, Changpeng Zhao, dressed in a suit, walked into the Seattle federal courthouse and admitted to violating the U.S. Bank Secrecy Act. He was not a Wall Street financial criminal, not a Ponzi scheme operator, and had not misappropriated a single penny of client assets. However, he did fail to register Binance in the United States despite knowing that Binance served American users. This single offense made him the first person in U.S. history to go to jail for violating BSA registration regulations. Four months in prison. A $4.3 billion fine. Resignation as Binance CEO. Now, he has published a book called *Binance Life*, subtitled "A Memoir of Luck, Resilience, and Protecting Users." Ray Dalio wrote a recommendation, BlackRock CEO Larry Fink said the book "shares key ideas for shaping one's life path," and even the King of Bhutan attended. Quite a spectacle. A memoir written by the person involved naturally carries his perspective and stance. What does it talk about, and what does it selectively omit? We open this book and try to glean some information from CZ's own account. It's filtered through his lens, but also rich in authentic details. From Well Water to Wall Street: A Standard Script for an Immigrant CZ was born in Qingkou Town, Ganyu County, Jiangsu Province. There was no running water; the nearest well was 300 meters away, and his mother carried it back and forth multiple times a day on a shoulder pole. There was no electricity; in the early grades of elementary school, he did his homework by kerosene lamp at night. When he was five years old, the adults in the village hung up light bulbs, and he felt like he had seen magic. These details are written with restraint in the book, without deliberate embellishment. But objectively, it does construct a clear narrative starting point: I came from the very bottom. At twelve, CZ immigrated to Canada with his family. His father bought a Datsun car for 400 Canadian dollars, a car that seemed destined to fall apart. His mother worked as a seamstress in a garment factory, leaving home at seven in the morning and returning at seven in the evening. On his fourteenth birthday, CZ applied for a job at McDonald's, earning 4.5 Canadian dollars per hour. At sixteen, he worked the night shift at a gas station, earning 12 Canadian dollars per hour. Later, after graduating from McGill University with a degree in computer science, he joined Bloomberg to develop futures trading systems. By twenty-five, he was leading a team of sixty people. When he left in 2005, his bonus and annual salary totaled $390,000. For a 28-year-old, this was a very respectable income. Then he went to Shanghai and co-founded a fintech company with five foreigners. The book details many aspects of doing business in China: getting orders by drinking, getting his foreign partners drunk to the point of vomiting, and renting the most expensive office space in the Hang Lung Plaza but still not making money. These passages are quite self-deprecating. The experience itself isn't unusual. A bright immigrant teenager changed his fate through education and part-time work, made money on Wall Street, and then ventured into emerging markets. Silicon Valley and Wall Street have thousands of such stories. What truly derailed CZ's life trajectory was a meal in the summer of 2013. Selling a House to Buy Crypto: Madness or Foresight? In July 2013, at a card game with friends in Shanghai, investor Cao Darong casually mentioned, "There's something new called Bitcoin, you could take a look." A few days later, CZ had dinner with angel investor Li Qiyuan. Li Qiyuan's advice was direct: "Convert 10% of your assets into Bitcoin. There's a small chance it will go to zero, and you'll lose 10%. But there's a high probability it will increase tenfold, and your net worth will double." CZ took it to heart. But what he did was far more aggressive than the "10%." He first left Fuxun, the company he had run for eight years. CEO and largest shareholder Mike Alfant gave him a choice: he could leave, but he had to give up all his shares. Eight years of accumulation, gone to zero. Then he started selling his apartment in Pudong, Shanghai. In January 2014, after receiving the proceeds from the sale, he began buying Bitcoin in large quantities. The average price was around $600. Less than a month later, Mt. Gox collapsed, and Bitcoin plummeted to $200. The money he had just earned from selling his apartment shrank by two-thirds. The book recounts how his mother often nagged, "Why can't you have a decent job like your sister? You're doing well at a big company, why do you insist on starting a business and getting involved with Bitcoin?"
